0:0.rendering: The words of the Teacher, son of David, king in Jerusalem.
0:0.note: This is the voice of a man who had everything yet still wrestled with the ache of life under the sun. The Teacher points us forward to the greater Son of David, Jesus, who alone can satisfy the restless heart.

0:1.rendering: "Smoke and vapor," says the Teacher, "utter smoke and vapor! Everything is smoke and vapor."
0:1.note: The Hebrew word hevel, repeated here like a sigh, means the breath you see on a cold morning—fleeting, insubstantial, gone in a moment. Qoheleth stares at life under the sun and calls it exactly what it is, so we won't pin our hopes on what can't hold them. This honest ache prepares the heart for the One who stepped into our vapor to give us what lasts forever.

0:2.rendering: What real gain does a person have from all the hard work they pour themselves into under the sun?
0:2.note: The Hebrew word for "gain" (yitron) carries the quiet ache of asking whether anything we sweat for actually lasts. Solomon stares at the treadmill of human effort and finds it empty—until we lift our eyes to the One who worked the hardest of all on a cross, then rose to give us a profit that death itself cannot touch.

0:3.rendering: One generation goes, and another comes, but the earth stays put forever.
0:3.note: Life feels like an endless parade of faces coming and going while the ground beneath us never budges. In the middle of that weary cycle, Jesus steps in as the One who is the same yesterday, today, and forever, anchoring our restless hearts to something that truly lasts.

0:4.rendering: The sun rises and the sun sets, then it hurries back to the place where it rises again.
0:4.note: This simple rhythm of the sun reminds us that the world keeps turning in its weary cycles, yet the One who made the sun stepped into our time to bring us rest that never wears out.

0:5.rendering: The wind blows to the south, then swirls back to the north; it spins and spins on its circuits, and the wind keeps returning on its rounds.
0:5.note: Like that restless wind chasing its own tail, we often spin in the same old ruts. Jesus invites us into His rest—the One who calmed the winds and whose Spirit blows where He wills, breaking the cycle of weary striving.

0:6.rendering: All the streams flow into the sea, yet the sea is never full. To the place where the streams flow, there they keep returning.
0:6.note: The Hebrew word for "streams" (nachal) carries the picture of a torrent that never quits its course. This verse shows the relentless, weary cycle of nature that leaves us unsatisfied. Only Jesus, the living water, can break the endless loop and fill the empty sea of our hearts.

0:7.rendering: All things are wearying; no one can put it into words. The eye is never satisfied with seeing, nor the ear filled with hearing.
0:7.note: The Preacher felt the bone-deep exhaustion of a world that keeps repeating itself without ever quite satisfying the soul. Jesus steps into that very weariness and says, “Come to me, all who are weary and burdened, and I will give you rest” (Matt 11:28). In him the restless heart finally finds the One who fills the eye and ear forever.

0:8.rendering: What has been is what will be, and what has been done is what will be done; there is nothing new under the sun.
0:8.note: The Preacher stares at the weary cycles of life and reminds us that chasing novelty apart from God is empty. Yet this same honest voice prepares our hearts for the One who makes all things new in Christ.

0:9.rendering: Is there anything of which one can say, "Look, this is brand new"? It has already existed long ago, before our time.
0:9.note: The Preacher cuts through our hunger for novelty with a single honest question. What feels fresh to us is usually just the same old ache wearing new clothes. Yet the One who makes all things new has stepped into our weary cycle, offering a hope that truly breaks the endless loop.

0:10.rendering: There is no lasting memory of those who came before us, and those who come after will not be remembered by those who follow them.
0:10.note: The Teacher stares straight into our hunger for legacy and says it slips away like sand through fingers. In a world that forgets so quickly, Jesus steps in as the One whose name will never be erased, inviting us to find our lasting place hidden in Him rather than in our own fleeting fame.

0:11.rendering: I, the Teacher, was king over Israel in Jerusalem.
0:11.note: The word for “Teacher” (Qoheleth) carries the weight of one who gathers and assembles people to hear hard truth. Even the richest, wisest king who ever lived looked at life under the sun and found it maddeningly empty; only Jesus, the greater Son of David, can fill that hollow place in our chests.

0:12.rendering: I devoted myself with all my heart to explore and examine by wisdom everything that is done under the sun. What a miserable task God has given to the children of man to keep them busy!
0:12.note: The Hebrew word for "search" here carries the weight of a relentless, almost painful investigation—like a man digging through the rubble of life looking for answers that keep slipping away. In our restless quest for meaning, we meet the One who searched the depths of our sin and suffering on the cross, so that our restless hearts could finally find their rest in him.

0:13.rendering: I have seen all the deeds that are done under the sun, and look—everything is vapor, a chasing after wind.
0:13.note: The Preacher stares honestly at life and calls it what it is: fleeting and frustrating. Only when we bring our restless hearts to Jesus, the One who entered our vapor-world to redeem it, does the chase finally find its rest.

0:14.rendering: What is crooked cannot be straightened, and what is lacking cannot be counted.
0:14.note: The Teacher stares at the twisted places in our world and in our own lives and says plainly: some damage runs too deep for human hands to fix. Only the One who hung crooked on a tree for us can make the bent straight again.

0:15.rendering: I said to myself, "Look, I have grown and increased in wisdom far beyond anyone who ruled over Jerusalem before me; my mind has absorbed vast wisdom and knowledge."
0:15.note: Qoheleth's relentless quest for wisdom under the sun left him empty-handed, for the fear of the Lord is the true beginning of knowledge. This honest confession ultimately drives us to Jesus, who is himself the wisdom of God and the only One who can satisfy the restless human heart.

0:16.rendering: So I set my heart to know wisdom and knowledge, madness and folly; I saw that this too was chasing after wind.
0:16.note: The Teacher threw himself into the quest for wisdom with everything he had, only to discover it couldn't fill the hollow place inside. That ache points us to Jesus, the Wisdom of God who doesn't just teach us truth but becomes our peace when all our striving leaves us empty.

0:17.rendering: Because the more wisdom you pile up, the more pain you carry; the more understanding you gain, the more sorrow you feel.
0:17.note: This verse stings with honesty. Solomon, who had more insight than anyone, discovered that wisdom without God only sharpens our awareness of the world's brokenness. True wisdom finds its rest in Christ, who was acquainted with grief yet invites us to trade our heavy knowledge for his easy yoke.

1:0.rendering: I said in my heart, "Come now, I will test you with pleasure. So enjoy yourself." But look, that too was vapor.
1:0.note: The Hebrew word for "vapor" (hevel) here is the book's signature term, evoking something fleeting like a breath or mist that you can't grasp. This experiment launches Qoheleth's quest to find meaning "under the sun," mirroring humanity's ancient drive to seize lasting good apart from God, a pattern that echoes the garden story and only finds resolution when the true King brings heaven and earth together in Jesus.

1:1.rendering: I said of laughter, "It's madness," and of pleasure, "What does it accomplish?"
1:1.note: The Hebrew word for "pleasure" here is the same root used for the "good" that God declares over creation in Genesis, yet the Teacher finds it hollow under the sun. This sets up a key tension in the unified biblical story: the good gifts of God's world lose their meaning when we chase them apart from the Giver.

1:2.rendering: I explored in my heart by intoxicating my body with wine, while guiding my mind with wisdom, and by grasping hold of folly, until I could see what is good for the children of humanity to do under the heavens during the few days of their lives.
1:2.note: The Hebrew word for "explored" here is the same one used in Genesis 2 for the man "working" the garden, hinting that Qohelet is conducting a grand experiment on what it means to be human under the sun. This search for lasting good amid life's fleeting days echoes the larger biblical story of humanity's quest for wisdom and joy in a broken world, a longing that finds its surprising resolution in the one who came to give life to the full.

1:3.rendering: I made my works great. I built houses for myself. I planted vineyards for myself.
1:3.note: In the thick of the Teacher's quest to find meaning under the sun, this line kicks off a catalog of grand achievements that echo the royal building projects of Israel's kings, especially Solomon, who was granted unparalleled wisdom to rule. Yet the repeated "for myself" hints at a self-focused pursuit that twists the image-of-God vocation to create and cultivate the world, revealing how even the best human endeavors, when detached from covenant relationship with the Creator, collapse into futility, a thread the story will ultimately resolve in the self-giving King who builds a new humanity.

1:4.rendering: I made myself gardens and parks, and I planted in them every kind of fruit tree.
1:4.note: In the ancient Near East, lush gardens and parks (the Hebrew word is pardes, the root of our word paradise) were symbols of a king's power to create order and abundance out of chaos. Ecclesiastes shows us the Teacher pursuing this Eden-like project in his quest for meaning under the sun, yet even this grand attempt at recreating paradise ultimately leaves him empty. The story keeps pointing forward to the true royal Son who will restore the garden for all humanity.

1:5.rendering: I made pools of water for myself, to water from them the forest sprouting with trees.
1:5.note: The Hebrew word for "pools" here is the same one used for the reservoirs Solomon built to irrigate his lush royal gardens, echoing the well-watered paradise of Eden and the river that flowed from God's presence. This fits into Ecclesiastes' larger quest to find lasting meaning under the sun, a search that the whole biblical story will ultimately fulfill when the river of life flows from a renewed temple in the new creation.

1:6.rendering: I bought male and female slaves, and I had slaves born in my house. I also had great possessions of herds and flocks, more than all who were in Jerusalem before me.
1:6.note: The Hebrew word for "acquired" or "bought" here echoes the language of Genesis, where humans are meant to rule and multiply as God's image, but the Teacher's relentless quest for meaning through possessions and human labor reveals the emptiness of trying to seize that role apart from the Creator. This fits into the Bible's larger pattern of exile from Eden's abundance, where our attempts to build our own mini-kingdoms only highlight our need for the true King who restores image-bearing life.

1:7.rendering: I also gathered silver and gold for myself, the treasures of kings and provinces. I acquired male and female singers for myself, and the delights of the sons of man: many concubines.
1:7.note: The word for "delights" here is ta'anug, which often carries the sense of luxurious pleasure or sensual indulgence, echoing the garden-like abundance of Eden but twisted into self-focused accumulation. This verse sits right in the middle of the Teacher's grand experiment with "everything under the sun," showing how even the best of human wisdom, wealth, and pleasure ends up as vapor, a pattern that only finds its resolution when the true King arrives to restore creation's delight in the New Testament.

1:8.rendering: So I became greater than all who were before me in Jerusalem. Yet my wisdom stayed with me.
1:8.note: The Teacher relentlessly pursues the ancient royal ideal of wisdom, wealth, and works, echoing the glory of Israel's kings like Solomon, only to expose how even the best human empire-building cannot bridge heaven and earth or escape the vapor-like futility of life under the sun.

1:9.rendering: I denied my eyes nothing they desired, and I refused my heart no pleasure. My heart took delight in all my toil, and this was my reward for all my toil.
1:9.note: The word for "toil" here is the Hebrew 'amal, which carries the heavy sense of exhausting, frustrating labor under the sun. This verse captures the Teacher's experiment in the wisdom tradition, pushing the image-of-God impulse to create and enjoy to its limit, only to discover that even the best pleasures and achievements cannot escape the vapor-like quality of life east of Eden.

1:10.rendering: I looked at all my projects that my hands had made, and at the hard work I had exerted to accomplish them. And look, it was all vapor, a chasing after wind. There was no lasting profit under the sun.
1:10.note: The Hebrew word hevel, often translated "vanity" or "meaningless," literally means "vapor" or "mist" something you can see but can't grasp. This moment in Ecclesiastes echoes the curse of Genesis 3, where human toil under the sun yields frustration instead of the fruitful abundance God intended. It sets up the Bible's larger story of how our restless search for meaning apart from the Creator ultimately points us toward the one who can make our work, joy, and life participate in something that lasts.

1:11.rendering: Then I turned to consider wisdom and madness and folly, for what can the man do who comes after the king, except what has already been done?
1:11.note: The word for "turned" here carries the idea of pivoting to examine life from a new angle, like the teacher is methodically testing every path under the sun. This moment fits into the book's larger pattern of exploring how human wisdom, even at its royal best, runs up against the same limits that every generation faces, a theme that resonates with the Bible's honest portrait of life east of Eden.

1:12.rendering: Then I saw that wisdom excels folly as light excels darkness.
1:12.note: The word for "excels" here is the Hebrew *yitron*, which means surplus or advantage, a favorite term in Ecclesiastes that keeps popping up like a refrain. This moment captures the Teacher's honest experiment with life under the sun, where wisdom really does offer a real edge over foolishness, yet both ultimately lead to the same end, death, pointing forward to the deeper resolution the biblical story finds in Jesus, who embodies wisdom that conquers the grave.

1:13.rendering: The wise person has eyes in his head, but the fool walks in darkness. Yet I also came to see that one fate comes to all of them.
1:13.note: The Hebrew word for "eyes in his head" plays on the idea of sight and insight, contrasting the wise who navigate life with clarity against the fool stumbling in the dark, yet both share the same ultimate fate of death. This observation sits at the heart of Ecclesiastes' exploration of life's enigmas under the sun, highlighting the limits of human wisdom and pointing forward to the New Testament's promise that Jesus, the true Wisdom of God, overcomes the grave and offers life beyond that shared fate.

1:14.rendering: Then I thought to myself, "What happens to the fool will happen to me also. So why have I been so exceedingly wise?" And I said in my heart, "This also is vapor."
1:14.note: The word for "vapor" here is hevel, the book's signature term that pictures something fleeting like a breath on a cold morning. Qohelet has just chased wisdom as the ultimate path to meaning, only to discover it ends in the same grave as folly, exposing how even our best human strategies can't overcome the shared human fate of death, a problem the Bible's larger story will ultimately address through a wisdom greater than Solomon's.

1:15.rendering: For the wise person dies just like the fool, and in the days to come they are both forgotten. How does the wise person die like the fool? This too is vapor.
1:15.note: The Hebrew word hevel, often translated vanity but literally meaning vapor or breath, paints a picture of something fleeting and insubstantial. This observation fits into the book's larger meditation on life under the sun, where even wisdom's advantages seem to dissolve in the face of death, echoing humanity's shared exile from the garden and pointing forward to the New Testament's hope that Jesus, the truly wise one, overcomes this vapor with resurrection life.

1:16.rendering: So I hated life, because what is done under the sun was grievous to me. For all is vapor, a chasing after wind.
1:16.note: The word translated "vapor" is hevel, the same name as Abel in Genesis, evoking something fleeting and insubstantial like breath on a cold morning. This moment in Ecclesiastes captures the deep frustration of life "under the sun" apart from God, echoing the curse of Genesis 3 where humanity's work and days are marked by futility and death. It sets up the book's larger quest to find meaning in a broken world, a longing that the story of Scripture ultimately answers in the one who entered our vapor-like existence to bring lasting life.

1:17.rendering: I hated all my toil that I toiled under the sun, which I must leave to the person who comes after me.
1:17.note: In the ancient Hebrew, the word for "hated" carries a visceral punch, revealing the Teacher's deep disillusionment with his life's achievements. This moment captures the larger biblical theme of humanity's frustrated search for lasting meaning apart from God, a pattern that echoes the curse of futility after Eden and points forward to the New Testament hope that our labor can be joined to something that truly endures in Jesus.

1:18.rendering: And who knows whether he will be a wise person or a fool? Yet he will rule over all the work that I have toiled for and skillfully made under the sun. This too is vapor.
1:18.note: The word translated "vapor" (hevel) is the book's signature term, evoking something fleeting, insubstantial, like a breath you can see on a cold morning. This verse captures the Preacher's anxiety about the fragility of legacy: all his image-of-God work of creating order and beauty can be inherited by someone who squanders it, a theme that echoes the fall in Genesis where human toil and its fruits become vulnerable to futility and death. Yet the larger biblical story moves toward a trustworthy Son who faithfully rules over all the Father's work, turning vapor into lasting new creation.

1:19.rendering: So I turned my heart to despair over all the hard work that I had done under the sun.
1:19.note: The key Hebrew word here for "despair" carries the idea of letting go or abandoning hope, a turning point where Qohelet, after chasing legacy and pleasure, confronts the futility of toil in a world fractured by death and injustice, echoing humanity's exile from the good order of Eden and our longing for a renewed creation where work finds its true purpose.

1:20.rendering: For there is a man whose toil is with wisdom, knowledge, and skill, yet he must leave his portion to someone who did not toil for it. This also is vapor and a chasing after wind.
1:20.note: The word for "toil" here is the same Hebrew term used in Genesis for humanity's painful labor after the garden, revealing how the curse of exile turns even wise work into something fleeting and unfair. This pattern of frustrated human effort echoes throughout the wisdom literature, pointing forward to a new kind of rest and inheritance that Jesus brings into the story.

1:21.rendering: For what does a person have for all his toil and the striving of his heart with which he toils under the sun?
1:21.note: The Hebrew word for "toil" here (amal) carries the weight of exhausting, anxiety-ridden labor, echoing the curse of Genesis 3 where the ground now demands sweat and struggle. This question sits at the heart of the Teacher's quest, exposing how our frantic pursuit of meaning through work and wisdom loops us back into the same vapor-like frustration that runs from Eden to exile, until a greater rest arrives in the story.

1:22.rendering: For all his days are full of pain, and his work is a vexation. Even at night his heart finds no rest. This also is vapor.
1:22.note: The Hebrew word 'amal' (toil or misery) and 'ra'ayon' (vexation or striving) paint a picture of humanity's frustrated labor under the sun, echoing the curse of Genesis 3 where the ground now yields thorns and thistles. This vapor-like existence sets the stage for the larger biblical story of how our restless work finds its true rest in the greater Son of David who offers an easy yoke.

1:23.rendering: There is nothing better for a person than to eat and drink and find satisfaction in their toil. This too, I saw, is from the hand of God.
1:23.note: Qohelet, the Preacher, has just chased every path of human wisdom, pleasure, and achievement only to find them all vapor, yet here he stumbles upon a quiet gift: simple enjoyment of food, drink, and work as something that comes directly from God's hand. This recurring refrain threads through the book like a counter-melody, hinting that even in our broken world under the sun, the Creator still invites his image-bearers to taste goodness right in the middle of the toil, a theme that echoes all the way to Jesus' table where he offers the bread and wine of a restored creation.

1:24.rendering: For who can eat or who can enjoy themselves apart from him?
1:24.note: The Hebrew word here for "enjoy" carries the sense of "seeing goodness," a key thread in Ecclesiastes. Qohelet has chased every form of pleasure under the sun only to discover that true enjoyment of life's gifts is not something we seize on our own, it's a gift that comes from the hand of the Creator himself. This longing for goodness that can't be grasped apart from God echoes throughout the biblical story, ultimately finding its answer in Jesus who offers the abundant life that truly satisfies.

1:25.rendering: For to the person who is good before him, God gives wisdom and knowledge and joy. But to the one who fails to please him, he gives the task of gathering and collecting, only to hand it over to the one who is good before God. This too is vapor, and a chasing after wind.
1:25.note: The Hebrew word for "good before him" carries the sense of being pleasing or aligned with God's way, a theme that echoes the image-of-God idea from Genesis. Qohelet sees this as part of the frustrating pattern of human life under the sun, yet it quietly anticipates the New Testament vision of inheritance given not by toil but by grace to those who belong to the true Son.

2:0.rendering: There is a season for everything, and a time for every matter under the heavens.
2:0.note: The Hebrew word for "season" here is et, which carries the idea of an appointed or divinely ordained time, revealing a rhythm woven into creation itself. This meditation on time's ordered cycles echoes the Bible's larger pattern of God bringing order out of chaos, inviting us to trust the Creator who holds all moments in his hands, a pattern that finds its ultimate expression in Jesus who stepped into our time to redeem it.

2:1.rendering: A time to give birth, and a time to die. A time to plant, and a time to pull up what is planted.
2:1.note: The Teacher opens his famous poem on seasons with birth and death, planting and uprooting, two image pairs that echo the opening pages of Genesis where humans are formed from the ground and given the task to cultivate it. This rhythm of giving and taking back reminds us that our lives unfold within the Creator's appointed times, a pattern that finds its deepest expression when the Word becomes flesh, enters our season of death, and rises as the firstfruits of new creation life.

2:2.rendering: a time to tear down and a time to build up, a time to kill and a time to heal, a time to tear apart and a time to sew together;
2:2.note: In the poem's rhythmic pairs, the Hebrew word for "tear down" (haras) and "build up" (banah) evoke the language of cities and houses being dismantled and reconstructed, a pattern that echoes throughout the Bible from the tower of Babel to the temple's destruction and rebuilding, and ultimately to Jesus who said he would tear down the temple and raise it in three days.

2:3.rendering: A time to weep, and a time to laugh. A time to mourn, and a time to dance.
2:3.note: This verse sits inside the poem of "times" that opens Ecclesiastes, painting the relentless rhythm of human experience under the sun. The Hebrew word for "time" (et) echoes the ordered seasons of Genesis 1, yet here it feels more like a mysterious dance between sorrow and joy, loss and celebration, reminding us that the biblical story never pretends life is only one or the other; it holds both in tension until the day when God will wipe away every tear and turn all mourning into dancing.

2:4.rendering: A time to throw away stones, and a time to gather stones together. A time to embrace, and a time to refrain from embracing.
2:4.note: In the ancient Near East, piling stones could mean clearing a field for planting or building a memorial, while scattering them might signal the end of a season or even an act of war, this verse weaves together rhythms of creation and disruption that echo the larger biblical pattern of order and chaos, exile and return. It invites us to see how the Preacher's meditation on time finds its surprising resolution in Jesus, who both scatters the stones of the temple and becomes the new cornerstone where heaven and earth finally embrace.

2:5.rendering: A time to seek, and a time to give up; a time to keep, and a time to throw away.
2:5.note: The Hebrew word for "seek" here carries the idea of searching with purpose, while "give up" implies letting go of what cannot be found, part of the poem's rhythm of opposites that reveals the limits of human control under the sun. This pattern of seasons echoes the Bible's larger story of exile and return, where God's people learn that there are times to hold tightly to covenant promises and times to release illusions of self-mastery, all pointing toward the one who brings every season to its fulfillment.

2:6.rendering: A time to tear and a time to mend, a time to be silent and a time to speak.
2:6.note: The Hebrew word for "tear" here carries the same imagery of ripping garments in grief or repentance that runs from Joseph's brothers through David's lament and into the prophets. This pairs with "mend" to show the rhythmic dance of disruption and repair woven into creation itself, a pattern that finds its deepest expression when the Word who spoke the universe into order becomes the one who is torn so that humanity can be mended.

2:7.rendering: a time to love and a time to hate, a time for war and a time for peace.
2:7.note: This verse sits at the climax of the poem in Ecclesiastes 3, where the Teacher catalogs every human activity under the sun as having its opposite "time" or season, revealing the frustrating order behind life's apparent chaos. The Hebrew word for "time" (et) echoes the larger biblical pattern of God's sovereign ordering of history, from creation's rhythms to the ultimate day when heaven and earth will be reconciled in Jesus, where love overcomes hate and peace finally triumphs over war.

2:8.rendering: What does the worker gain from all his toil?
2:8.note: This question sits at the heart of Ecclesiastes' meditation on the cycles of time, echoing the poem's refrain of "under the sun." The Hebrew word for "gain" or "profit" (yitron) carries the image of something left over or that sticks out, like excess thread, yet the Teacher finds only vapor. It pulls us into the larger biblical tension between humanity's image-of-God calling to work the ground and the frustrating futility that entered the world through the curse in Genesis 3.

2:9.rendering: I have seen the task that God has given to the children of humanity to keep them occupied.
2:9.note: The Hebrew word for "task" here is the same one used for the grueling labor of Israel's slavery in Egypt, now reframed as the shared human condition of striving under the sun. This fits into the Bible's larger pattern of humanity's restless work in a fractured world, a thread that finds its resolution when Jesus invites the weary to take on his easy yoke and enter God's ultimate rest.

2:10.rendering: He has made everything beautiful in its time. He has also put eternity into their hearts, yet so that they cannot find out what God has done from beginning to end.
2:10.note: The word for "eternity" here is olam, which can mean both endless time and the hidden, distant future. The Teacher is noticing how God has wired humans with this restless awareness that life is part of a vast, beautiful design, yet we cannot fully grasp it. This tension echoes throughout the Bible's story, from humanity's longing in the garden to the ultimate unveiling of God's purposes in Jesus, where heaven and earth finally come together in full view.

2:11.rendering: I know that there is nothing better for them than to be glad and to do good in their lives.
2:11.note: The word translated "good" here is the Hebrew "tov," the same word that echoes through the creation story as God repeatedly calls his world "good." Amid the relentless cycles of time in Ecclesiastes, this moment of insight celebrates the simple joy of participating in God's original tov, a thread that runs from Eden's garden through Israel's story and finds its fullest expression when Jesus invites us into the abundant, good life of the new creation.

2:12.rendering: And also, for every person to eat and drink and see good in all their toil, this is God's gift.
2:12.note: The Hebrew word for "gift" here is mattah, the same term used for the divine inheritance of the land given to Israel. In a book that stares hard at the fleeting vapor of life under the sun, this verse quietly insists that simple enjoyment of food, drink, and work is not a random perk but a tangible sign of God's generous overlap of heaven and earth, a thread that runs all the way to Jesus inviting us to receive the kingdom like a child at a table.

2:13.rendering: I know that everything God does will last forever. Nothing can be added to it, and nothing can be taken away from it. God has done it this way so that people will stand in awe before him.
2:13.note: The Hebrew word for "forever" here is olam, carrying the sense of something hidden from human view across time. In a book that relentlessly shows how human striving under the sun leads to futility, this verse reveals God's works as the one stable thread that can't be improved or undone, inviting awe instead of control, a pattern that echoes through the Bible's story of a faithful Creator whose purposes hold the chaotic world together.

2:14.rendering: What has been is now, and what is to be has already been, and God seeks out what has been driven away.
2:14.note: The Teacher circles back to the idea that time is not a straight line of progress but a repeating pattern under the sun, yet he adds a fascinating twist: God himself "seeks out" what has been "driven away" or "pursued into the past." This hints at God's commitment to gather up every scattered moment of his creation, a thread that later blooms in the New Testament hope that in Jesus, God is reconciling and summing up all things.

2:15.rendering: I saw something else under the sun: in the place of justice there was wickedness, and in the place of righteousness there was wickedness.
2:15.note: The Hebrew word for "place" (maqom) here is the same one used for sacred spaces like the tabernacle or temple, creating a stark irony: the very locations meant to uphold God's order of justice and righteousness are instead filled with the opposite. This observation fits Ecclesiastes' relentless catalog of life's "under the sun" frustrations, part of the larger biblical pattern where human systems fail to reflect heaven's order, a problem that deepens until Jesus arrives as the true righteous judge who merges heaven and earth.

2:16.rendering: I said in my heart, God will judge the righteous and the wicked. For there is a time for every matter and every deed there.
2:16.note: The word for "time" here is the same "et" that frames the poem in verses 1-8, suggesting this isn't random chaos but a purposeful rhythm under heaven. Ecclesiastes wrestles with the apparent futility of life "under the sun," yet this verse quietly echoes the larger biblical thread of covenant justice: the God who orders seasons and human action is also the Judge who will one day set all things right, a hope that finds its yes in Jesus, the one appointed to judge the living and the dead.

2:17.rendering: I said in my heart concerning the sons of man, that God is testing them to show them that they are but beasts.
2:17.note: The Hebrew word for "testing" here carries the idea of sorting or distinguishing, as a refiner separates what is true from what is false. This verse sits in the center of Ecclesiastes' poem about times and seasons, revealing humanity's shared fate with animals in death and reminding us that the whole biblical story wrestles with our creaturely limits until the resurrection hope in Jesus redefines what it means to be image-bearing humans.

2:18.rendering: For the fate of the sons of man and the fate of the beast is the same fate. As one dies, so dies the other. All have one breath, and man has no advantage over the beast. For all is vapor.
2:18.note: The Hebrew word for vapor, hevel, echoes the book's opening poem and paints everything as fleeting and insubstantial. This verse pulls the rug out from under any illusion of human superiority, showing that in death we're leveled with the animals, a sobering thread that runs through the wisdom literature until the resurrection hope in Jesus reframes our shared mortality.

2:19.rendering: All go to one place. All are from the dust, and all return to dust.
2:19.note: This verse echoes the Genesis creation story where humanity is formed from the adamah (ground) and returns to it in death, highlighting our shared creaturely fate with the animals. It sits in the larger biblical pattern of dust-to-dust mortality that only finds its resolution when Jesus, the new Adam, rises from the grave and begins the work of new creation.

2:20.rendering: Who knows whether the spirit of the sons of man goes upward and the spirit of the beast goes down into the earth?
2:20.note: The Hebrew word 'ruach' here means both 'breath' and 'spirit,' and the Teacher is probing the mystery of what happens after death, wondering if humans and animals share the same fate since both return to dust. This question echoes the Genesis 2-3 story of humanity formed from the ground with God's breath of life, highlighting our shared creatureliness in a world marked by death and pointing forward to the New Testament's hope that the same Spirit who raised Jesus will one day renew all creation.

2:21.rendering: So I saw that there is nothing better for a person than to rejoice in his work, because that is his portion. For who can bring him to see what will be after him?
2:21.note: The Preacher lands on this refrain again and again: enjoy your toil as your "portion" (heleq), the same word used for the Levites' inheritance in the land. In a world of endless cycles and unknowable futures, this small, concrete joy becomes a taste of the good order God designed for his image-bearers, a thread that later blooms into Jesus' invitation to abundant life lived fully in the present under the Father's care.

3:0.rendering: I looked again and saw all the acts of oppression that are done under the sun. Look, the tears of the oppressed, with no one to comfort them. Power is on the side of their oppressors, with no one to comfort them.
3:0.note: This raw cry against unchecked power and the tears of the oppressed echoes the exodus story where God hears the groaning of slaves and comes to deliver them. In the Bible's larger pattern, it highlights humanity's failure to image God faithfully, yet it sets the stage for the coming king who will one day wipe away every tear and establish justice.

3:1.rendering: So I considered the dead who have already died more fortunate than the living who are still alive.
3:1.note: In the thick of life's oppression and injustice, the Teacher looks at the endless cycle of toil and concludes the dead are better off than the living, a raw observation that echoes the Bible's honest wrestling with the curse of futility and exile from Eden's shalom.

3:2.rendering: But better than both of them is the one who has not yet been, who has not seen the evil deeds that are done under the sun.
3:2.note: The Teacher's bleak logic spirals deeper here, ranking the never-born as better off than the living or the dead because they escape the endless oppression and evil cycling "under the sun." This fits Ecclesiastes' larger pattern of exposing how life in a broken world feels like futile vapor, a theme that resonates with the biblical story's honest wrestling with exile-like conditions until the arrival of the one who overcomes the world and brings new creation beyond the sun.

3:3.rendering: I saw that all the hard work and all the skillful effort people put into their projects comes from their desire to be envied by their neighbor. This too is vapor, a chasing after wind.
3:3.note: The word often translated "rivalry" or "envy" here is qinah, which carries the idea of jealous passion, the same root used for God's own fierce love for Israel. Ecclesiastes peels back the layers of our drive to succeed and finds this same human impulse underneath, another fleeting breath in a world of vapor that God will one day renew through the self-giving work of Jesus.

3:4.rendering: The fool folds his hands and eats his own flesh.
3:4.note: This vivid little proverb plays on the Hebrew word for "flesh" (basar), which can mean both literal meat and one's own body. The fool who refuses to work ends up consuming his own life in idleness and self-destruction, a picture that echoes the Bible's wider theme of how rejecting the Creator's good order over creation leads us to unravel the very life God designed us to enjoy.

3:5.rendering: Better is a handful of restfulness than two fists full of toil and chasing after wind.
3:5.note: The Teacher spots a wisdom pattern that runs through the whole Bible: grasping for more often leads to emptiness, while contentment is a form of shalom that echoes the seventh-day rest of creation. This same tension shows up later when Jesus invites the weary to take his easy yoke instead of the endless striving that leaves us chasing vapor.

3:6.rendering: Again I saw something pointless under the sun: there was one person, and he had no second, no son or brother either. Yet there was no end to all his toil, and his eyes were never satisfied with riches. "For whom am I toiling," he says, "and depriving myself of good?" This also is vapor, a chasing after wind.
3:6.note: The Hebrew word hevel, often translated "vanity" or "meaningless," literally means "vapor" or "breath," evoking something fleeting and ungraspable. This observation fits into the book's larger exploration of life under the sun, where the relentless pursuit of wealth without meaningful relationships echoes humanity's fractured image-of-God design for community, a theme that finds its resolution in Jesus who restores us to covenant love with God and one another.

3:7.rendering: There is one who is alone, with no second beside him. He has neither son nor brother, yet there is no end to all his toil. His eyes are never satisfied with riches. "For whom am I toiling," he asks, "and depriving myself of good?" This also is vapor, a chasing after wind.
3:7.note: The Hebrew word "hevel" (vapor) captures the fleeting, insubstantial nature of this solitary pursuit of wealth, echoing the book's opening theme of life's enigmas under the sun. This image of the isolated striver connects to the Bible's larger pattern of human exile from Eden's relational harmony, where self-focused toil without covenant community or future hope leads only to emptiness, a thread that finds its resolution in Jesus who restores us to true communion with God and others.

3:8.rendering: Two are better than one, because they have a good reward for their toil.
3:8.note: The Teacher of Ecclesiastes, after exploring the emptiness of isolated human effort under the sun, lands on this simple proverb that echoes God's original design in Genesis 2:18, where it was not good for the human to be alone. The Hebrew word for reward here hints at something shared and lasting, a small but vital thread of goodness that points forward to the New Testament vision of the church as a community where heaven-and-earth overlap through mutual love and burden-bearing, ultimately fulfilled in Jesus who calls us into life together.

3:9.rendering: For if they fall, one will lift up his companion. But woe to the one who falls and has no one to lift him up.
3:9.note: The Hebrew word for "fall" here carries the idea of stumbling or collapsing under life's burdens, echoing the Bible's larger pattern of human frailty and the need for rescue. This verse sits in the middle of the Teacher's exploration of life's vapor-like futility, highlighting how the image-of-God design for partnership and covenant community counters isolation, a thread that runs from Eden's "not good to be alone" all the way to the mutual lifting up we see in the early church and in Jesus, the ultimate companion who raises the fallen.

3:10.rendering: Also, if two lie down together, they keep warm. But how can one person keep warm alone?
3:10.note: In the thick of exploring life's vapor-like futility, this proverb highlights the ancient wisdom that humans were made for partnership, echoing the image-of-God design in Genesis where it's not good for the human to be alone. The Hebrew word for "warm" carries a subtle double meaning of both physical heat and emotional comfort, pointing forward to the covenant community and ultimately the mutual indwelling Jesus offers his followers.

3:11.rendering: Though one person may be overpowered, two can stand their ground together. And a threefold cord is not quickly torn apart.
3:11.note: This proverb sits in a section exploring the vapor-like futility of isolation, yet it quietly celebrates the surprising strength that emerges when humans bind themselves together. The Hebrew word for "cord" (chut) evokes the twisted strands of a rope, a tangible image of covenant-like partnership that echoes God's own design for image-bearing community, and later finds its ultimate fulfillment in the unbreakable threefold cord of Father, Son, and Spirit.

3:12.rendering: Better is a poor but wise youth than an old and foolish king who no longer knows how to take advice.
3:12.note: The Hebrew word for "youth" here is na'ar, which often carries the sense of a servant or underdog, while the king has grown deaf to counsel, a classic image of foolish power. This small proverb fits into Ecclesiastes' larger meditation on the vapor-like cycles of human striving, where even the greatest status can crumble, pointing forward to the biblical pattern that true wisdom and kingship come not from age or throne but from the servant who listens to God's voice.

3:13.rendering: For he came out of prison to become king, even though in his own kingdom he was born poor.
3:13.note: The Hebrew word for "prison" here (beit hasurim) literally means "house of bonds," evoking the image of Joseph rising from the pit and prison to rule Egypt, a pattern of reversal that echoes the Bible's larger story of exile and return. This verse highlights how God's upside-down kingdom often lifts the lowly from bondage into authority, a thread that finds its ultimate expression in Jesus, who went from the lowest place to the highest throne.

3:14.rendering: I saw all the living who move about under the sun, along with that youth who would take the place of the king: he was the second one to arise from among the people.
3:14.note: The Hebrew word for "second" (sheni) here echoes a recurring biblical pattern of the younger or unexpected one rising to power, like Jacob over Esau or David over his brothers. In the story of Ecclesiastes, this fleeting observation about political succession highlights the endless cycles of human striving under the sun, a theme that builds toward the deeper wisdom Jesus brings as the true king who breaks those cycles of vanity and exile.

3:15.rendering: There was no end to all the people who were before him, yet those who came after could not rejoice in him. This also is vapor, a chasing after wind.
3:15.note: The word for "vapor" here is hevel, the same breath-like mist that gives the book its name, capturing how even massive crowds and political success evaporate into nothing. In the story of the Bible, this relentless cycle of rulers rising and falling echoes humanity's fractured image-bearing rule, a problem that finds its surprising resolution when the true Son of David steps into the story to rule with wisdom that doesn't fade.
